name: Cockatrice
description: The cockatrice is a legendary creature, reputed to kill with a single glance or touch. Originating in European legends, particularly during the medieval period, the cockatrice is often depicted as a serpent-like being with the head of a rooster. It is believed to be born from a rooster's egg incubated by a serpent or toad, a bizarre and unnatural act that contributes to its monstrous nature. The creature's deadly gaze, known as the 'death stare', is its most feared attribute, capable of petrifying or instantly killing any living being that meets its eyes. Its breath is also said to be poisonous, further adding to its dangerous reputation. The cockatrice is often associated with disease and pestilence, seen as a harbinger of misfortune and a symbol of evil. Throughout history, various methods have been proposed to defeat the cockatrice, including the use of weasels, roosters, or mirrors to reflect its deadly gaze back upon itself.
subtitle: A deadly serpent-rooster hybrid.
appearance: A serpentine body with the head, legs, and sometimes wings of a rooster. Often depicted with a crown or crest on its head, signifying its kingly, albeit monstrous, status. Its scales are typically a greenish-brown hue, and its eyes glow with an unnatural light.
habitat: Dark and desolate places, such as caves, ruins, and deserts. Often associated with areas contaminated by evil or disease.
behavior: Aggressive and territorial, the cockatrice is a solitary creature that fiercely guards its domain. It is said to be highly intelligent and cunning, capable of ambushing its prey with deadly precision. It avoids light and prefers the shadows, emerging primarily at night to hunt.

keyMyths.title: The Cockatrice and the Basilisk
keyMyths.description: Often confused with the basilisk, the cockatrice is frequently mentioned in bestiaries and medieval literature alongside its serpentine cousin. Both creatures share the deadly gaze and poisonous nature, but the cockatrice is distinguished by its rooster-like features and unique origin story involving a rooster's egg.

keyMyths.title: The Cockatrice in European Folklore
keyMyths.description: Tales of the cockatrice appear in various European folklore traditions, often serving as cautionary tales about the dangers of unnatural acts and the consequences of meddling with the natural order. The creature's appearance is frequently linked to outbreaks of disease or periods of misfortune, reinforcing its association with evil and destruction.

keyMyths.title: The Legend of the Cockatrice in the Bible
keyMyths.description: While the King James Version of the Bible mentions the 'cockatrice', this is often considered a mistranslation of other venomous creatures. The term was used to describe a dangerous serpent, contributing to the creature's fearful reputation and association with evil in religious contexts. The cockatrice is also seen as a symbol of the antichrist.

sources.author: White, T.H.
sources.work: The Book of Beasts: Being a Translation from a Latin Bestiary of the Twelfth Century
sources.description: A translation of a medieval bestiary, providing insight into the historical understanding and depiction of mythical creatures like the cockatrice.

sources.author: Pliny the Elder
sources.description: An early source that describes similar creatures, though the exact term 'cockatrice' came later, Pliny describes venomous serpents with deadly gazes, contributing to the later legend.
